lesson.menu_desc: Review of Block 2: persuasion, questioning research and defending data in a mock panel debate -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Review -- The Scientific Debate
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 10 -- The Scientific Debate

listenings.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to today's scientific debate. As your moderator, my job is simple: to keep the discussion fair, to give each speaker equal time, and to make sure that every claim is supported by evidence. The motion for today is that functional ingredients should be approved for the market within twelve months. One side will argue in favor of the motion, and the other side will rebut it with a counterpoint. I would like to remind both teams of three rules. First, be persuasive, but stay polite. Second, when you disagree, do not attack the person; question the data instead. And third, every argument must be valid, which means it must be logical and supported by reliable research. Please articulate your points clearly, and remember that a good debater listens as carefully as he or she speaks. Let us begin with the opening statement in favor of the motion. You have two minutes. The floor is yours.

Thank you both for a strong debate. As the judge, I would like to share a little feedback. The team in favor of the motion made a very persuasive case. They articulated their main argument clearly, and they used solid evidence to support every claim, so their reasoning was valid from start to finish. The team against the motion also did well. Their counterpoint about the limited sample was a fair and reasonable concern, and they were right to question the methodology rather than to attack their opponents. If I had to choose one thing to improve, I would say this: when you rebut an argument, give your own evidence immediately, do not only point out the weakness in the other side. Overall, both teams stayed calm under pressure and defended their data with confidence. That is exactly what a good scientific debate looks like. Well done to everyone, and thank you for listening.

extra_audio.text: First, the moderator reads the motion and explains the rules. Next, the first team articulates its argument and gives the evidence. After that, the other team offers a counterpoint and questions the data. Then, each side has a chance to rebut and to clarify. Finally, the judge gives feedback and decides which case was more persuasive.
